retrieved_docs.title: Vesicovaginal Fistula -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Outline the typical presentation of a patient with vesicovaginal fistula. Review the risk factors for developing vesicovaginal fistula. Describe the physical exam findings associated with vesicovaginal fistula. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Vesicovaginal Fistula -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Vesicovaginal fistula is an anomalous communication between the bladder and vagina, resulting in continuous urine leakage through the vagina. This condition occurs most commonly after obstetrical and gynecological injury. To avoid significant morbidity, distress, and psychosocial issues associated with vesicovaginal fistula, this condition must be accurately diagnosed and treated in a timely fashion. This activity reviews the evaluation and treatment of vesicovaginal fistulas and highlights the role of the healthcare team in managing patients with this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: Vesicovaginal Fistula -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Transvaginal ultrasonography may clearly visualize the exact size, site, and course of the fistula. Transvaginal sonographic evaluation is well-tolerated with a low side-effect profile and more instructive than some other conventional investigations. [15] However, sonographic findings are dependent on operator skill and experience and are infrequently used in the evaluation of fistulas. Cystoscopy is of particular help and can be used to identify the exact location of the fistula tract in the bladder. A small ureteric catheter or pediatric foley catheter can be passed through a suspected fistula tract to determine if and where it enters the vagina. Vaginoscopy can be performed in the office using the cystoscope and may aid in the localization of the fistula opening on the vaginal side. It is easily performed in the office and is well tolerated by most patients. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Vesicovaginal Fistula -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The diagnosis is traditionally established utilizing clinical evaluation and dye testing. In addition to the methylene blue dye test described above, a "double-dye" test is also generally performed. This is accomplished by having the patient take oral phenazopyridine just before coming into the office. [13] A tampon or gauze is inserted into the vagina. Once the bladder is sufficiently full (indicated by an urge to urinate), the tampon is removed and inspected for orange-staining, and, if present, a genitourinary tract fistula is diagnosed. This could encompass a ureterovaginal or vesicovaginal fistula. The bladder is then back-filled with dilute methylene blue, as described above. If there is only orange-staining and no blue-staining of the tampon, a ureterovaginal fistula is most likely. If there is both orange and blue staining on the gauze then, a vesicovaginal fistula is likely but a concomitant uretetovaginal fistula cannot be entirely excluded. This can be further confirmed with imaging. Transvaginal ultrasonography, cystoscopy, and contrast studies may be utilized. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Vesicovaginal Fistula -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: A vesicovaginal fistula is an abnormal communication between the bladder and vagina, resulting in continuous urine leakage through the vagina. [1] This condition occurs most commonly after obstetrical and gynecological injury. In the developed world, the most common cause of vesicovaginal fistula is gynecological surgery. In North America, bladder injury during a hysterectomy is the most common cause. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Vesicovaginal Fistula -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Postoperative or postpartum patients 1 to 2 weeks out from pelvic surgery or obstructed labor with a complaint of abnormal vaginal fluid leakage should be properly evaluated to determine the accurate diagnosis, and the clinician should have a high index of suspicion for a vesicovaginal fistula. The diagnosis is traditionally established utilizing clinical evaluation and tampon dye testing. A high creatinine level of the vaginal fluid can confirm the discharge is urine, but the clinician should be cautious in interpreting this as other types of urinary incontinence can also result in urine collecting in the vagina. Office cystoscopy and imaging studies are also often utilized in the evaluation. The differential diagnosis of postoperative or postpartum abnormal vaginal fluid leakage includes infection, inflammation, malignancy, urgency or stress urinary incontinence, or other urogenital abnormalities. [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Vesicovaginal Fistula -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Contrast studies, such as a multiphasic CT urogram, are recommended before proceeding with surgical repair to identify concomitant ureteral fistulas or abnormalities. More invasive and advanced techniques are generally not required but may include endoanal ultrasound or subtraction magnetic resonance fistulography. [17] Simple vesicovaginal fistulas are singular, small in size (<0.5 cm), and found in non-radiated patients with no evidence of malignancy involvement. A vesicovaginal fistula is considered intermediate in size if measured at 0.5 to 2.5 cm. Complex vesicovaginal fistulas are large in size (>2.5 cm), and associated with chronic disease, post-radiation, or failed previous fistula repair. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Vesicovaginal Fistula -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: The classic clinical presentation of a patient with vesicovaginal fistula is continuous urinary incontinence after recent pelvic surgery. The severity of symptomology and timing of presentation is variable. Postoperative patients with a vesicovaginal fistula are usually easily diagnosed by the complaint of urine leaking through the vagina 7 to 12 days after gynecological or pelvic surgery. The diagnosis of a vesicovaginal fistula can be made in an outpatient clinical setting. Once the diagnosis of vesicovaginal fistula has been established, a comprehensive diagnostic workup should be pursued to not overlook a concomitant ureteral injury or other fistulae. While a pelvic surgeon (gynecologist, urologist, or urogynecologist) is almost always involved in caring for a patient with a vesicovaginal fistula, an entire interprofessional team of specialists is required for a successful repair. This team often includes nurses, obstetricians, pharmacists, radiologists, and anesthesiologists. Despite an optimistic prognosis for patients receiving proper treatment, efforts should be focused on the prevention of vesicovaginal formation. This includes prioritizing women’s reproductive health, emergency obstetric services, and properly trained pelvic surgeons in the global medical community. An effort should also be made to educate the general public on the prevalence and prevention of this devastating problem. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Vesicovaginal Fistula -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Considering the suspicious genitourinary fistulas, the first critical point to address is the patient's history. Symptom development following a recent surgery should be explicitly evaluated. Standard questions, including cancer radiation, obstructed labor, similar to the discussed patient, and trauma, should be raised. Specific symptom characteristics, including the leakage volume and or vaginal fluid color and smell, should be noted. The critical notion is to differentiate the urogenital fistula-related symptoms from other possible causalities of urinary incontinence. Accordingly, patients with urogenital fistula admit to changing heavy pads more frequently. Physical examination should be started with a split speculum examination. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Vesicovaginal Fistula -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The etiology of this condition varies and can be categorized into congenital or acquired. Acquired vesicovaginal fistula can be further divided into obstetric surgical, malignant, radiation, and miscellaneous categories. Congenital vesicovaginal fistula is very rare and typically associated with other urogenital malformations. Vesicovaginal fistula occurs most commonly after obstetrical and gynecological injury. In the developed world, the most common cause of vesicovaginal fistula is gynecological surgery. In North America, bladder injury during a hysterectomy is the most common cause. In developing countries, obstructed labor and the resulting pressure necrosis are the most common causes of vesicovaginal fistulas. Poor socioeconomic status, malnourishment, low literacy rate, early marriage and childbearing, and inadequate obstetrical care are important risk factors for developing a vesicovaginal fistula in the developing world. [1] Miscellaneous acquired causes of this condition include retroperitoneal, vascular, or pelvic surgery, urologic or gynecologic instrumentation, infectious and inflammatory diseases, sexual trauma, vaginal laser procedures, external violence, and vaginal foreign bodies. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Vesicovaginal Fistula -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: The classic clinical presentation of a patient with a vesicovaginal fistula is continuous urinary incontinence after recent pelvic surgery. The severity of symptomology and timing of presentation is variable. Postoperative patients with a vesicovaginal fistula are usually easily diagnosed by the complaint of urine leaking through the vagina 7 to 12 days after gynecological or pelvic surgery. The timing of symptomology is likely related to tissue necrosis following obstructed prolonged labor or tissue strangulated by sutures placed during pelvic surgery.

retrieved_docs.title: Vesicovaginal Fistula -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: During the early post-traumatic phase, proceeding with physical examination under anesthesia and application of a dye test would be reasonable. Moreover, complex fistulas involving ureters are possible. To perform dye tests, several sterile fluid options, including sterile infant formula or indigo carmine, exist. The urethra would be compressed with the application of a gauze sponge while the bladder is being filled with colored fluid. In the absence of leakage over the placed tampon or cotton swabs, the next step is to ask the patient to cough. (to induce Valsalva maneuver). The color of the staining would discriminate the source of potential fistula. That said, if the tip of the tampon is blue-stained, a vesicovaginal fistula is suspected. However, clear fluid wetness is compatible with the presence of a uterovaginal fistula. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Vesicovaginal Fistula -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: The diagnosis of a vesicovaginal fistula can be made in an outpatient clinical setting. A thorough evaluation of the size, number, and location of the fistula is important before curative surgery is undertaken. Physical examination, including a lighted speculum exam, of the fistula and surrounding structures, is extremely important to evaluate for acute inflammation, infection, edema, necrosis, or other bladder or vaginal pathology. Surgical correction of the defect should be postponed for up to 2 to 3 months if needed to allow for recovery and optimal tissue health. However, if there is no evidence of inflammation, and the tissue appears healthy, the surgical correction does not need to be postponed. [8] Office cystoscopy and contrast studies, such as computed tomography (CT) urogram, are generally used to aid in the diagnosis of additional injuries (such as a concomitant ureteral injury or fistula) and for surgical planning.

retrieved_docs.title: Vesicovaginal Fistula -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: The primary complication of vesicovaginal fistula surgery is recurrent fistula formation. Urinary frequency and urgency most commonly arise in the acute perioperative setting. Urgency and stress urinary incontinence are recognized as long-term complications of vesicovaginal repair. In obstetric causes for fistula formation where the sphincter mechanism is injured, stress urinary incontinence is common. Anticholinergic medication can be used to reduce symptoms for urgency symptoms. Stress urinary incontinence can be treated with the usual treatment for that disorder. Failure and recurrence occurred in 30% of vesicovaginal repair cases. In recurrent cases, consider a delay of 2 to 3 months from the previous attempted repair. Utilizing the interposition of flaps as a surgical technique should be considered to increase the chances of a successful repair. [23]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 2 -- Community 825
retrieved_docs.content: Closure of vesicovaginal fistula by transvesical and vaginal approach and closure of vesicovaginal fistula by vaginal approach are both therapeutic interventions directly linked to the condition ناسور مهبلي. The condition ناسور مهبلي is mapped to the nonspecific genital‑organ finding تدلٍ غير محدد في الأعضاء التناسلية. This nonspecific finding falls under the broader category of prolapse of female genital organs, a clinical entity that also encompasses تدلٍّ رحمي. Recognizing that vesicovaginal fistula repair addresses a specific manifestation within the spectrum of female genital organ prolapse and related nonspecific genital findings can aid clinicians in comprehensive assessment and management of pelvic floor disorders.

All of these conditions share the common pathophysiological mechanism of organ descent or herniation into the vaginal canal, and their management frequently involves direct intervention on the vaginal walls, correction of obstructive changes, and prevention or treatment of fistulous sequelae.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2845
retrieved_docs.content: Vesicovaginal fistula with involvement of urinary continence mechanism following normal delivery is classified as a form of other pregnancy and delivery including normal, indicating that this specific type of fistula is considered among the broader spectrum of pregnancy‑related and delivery‑related conditions that can occur even after an uncomplicated, normal birth. Recognizing this classification helps clinicians understand that urinary continence problems associated with vesicovaginal fistula may arise in the context of routine deliveries, prompting appropriate postpartum evaluation and management to address continence impairment and improve patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2815
retrieved_docs.content: Urogenital disorders (اضطراب بولي تناسلي) encompass a range of conditions affecting the urinary and reproductive systems, and a urogenital fistula (ناسور بولي تناسلي) is a specific manifestation that falls within this broader category.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 2187
retrieved_docs.content: Posterior repair of vagina is a surgical intervention that directly involves the vagina, aiming to restore its structural integrity. Vesicovaginal fistula with involvement of urinary continence mechanism following normal delivery is a pathological condition that is located in the vagina, often compromising urinary continence after childbirth. Both concepts underscore the clinical importance of the vagina as a site for therapeutic repair and as a potential location of obstetric complications affecting urinary function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 127
retrieved_docs.content: يتضمن السبيل التناسلي لدى الإناث (fistula involving the female reproductive tract). Treatments for bladder diverticulum (علاج القيلة المثانية) and rectal diverticulum (علاج قيلة مستقيمية) are both linked to the development of فَتْق (fistula), highlighting a common complication across these therapeutic approaches. Collectively, these entities illustrate the interconnected nature of female pelvic organ prolapse, its anatomical sites, associated disorders, and the spectrum of reconstructive and reparative procedures employed in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 658
retrieved_docs.content: Closure of vesicovaginal fistula by transvesical and vaginal approach and closure of vesicovaginal fistula by vaginal approach are both surgical interventions that directly address ناسور مهبلي (vaginal fistula). In clinical terminology, ناسور مهبلي is also mapped to تدلٍ غير محدد في الأعضاء التناسلية, indicating that the condition may present as an unspecified finding within the genital organs. These relationships highlight that the described closure techniques are specific treatment options for vaginal fistula, a condition that can manifest with non‑specific genital symptoms.

Collectively, these relationships illustrate how pelvic and lower‑genitourinary conditions—including fistula formation, urethrocele, various organ prolapses, and bladder herniation—are interconnected through shared anatomical sites (the vagina and vaginal wall), procedural classifications, and common pathological features such as انسدال and فَتْق, thereby providing clinicians with an integrated framework for patient‑centered management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 653
retrieved_docs.content: The repair of urethrovaginal fistula is identified as a specific subcategory of **علاج الناسور المهبلي**, highlighting it as a distinct component within the broader therapeutic strategy for vaginal fistulas. Collectively, these surgical techniques aim to correct diverse vaginal and adjacent‑organ fistulas, restore normal anatomy, and reestablish functional continence and sexual health in affected patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 2 -- Community 1212
retrieved_docs.content: The formation of a urethrovaginal fistula, also referred to as urethrovaginaal; fistel, is identified as a structural abnormality of the vagina (هيكلة المهبل) that originates at the urethra. The urethra is the primary finding site for this fistula, and clinical evaluation often reveals urethral obstruction (تدلّي الإحليل) and urethral blockage (اِنسِداد الإحليل) as associated findings. The presence of urethral obstruction is linked to the development of a fistula (فَتْق), indicating that obstructive processes in the urethra can predispose to fistulous communication between the urethra and vagina. Additionally, the morphological feature of a fistula (فَتْق) is also observed in the treatment of bladder diverticulum (علاج القيلة المثانية), suggesting that fistulous tracts may arise as a direct consequence of therapeutic interventions for diverticular disease. Recognizing the anatomical site, associated obstructive pathology, and the broader context of fistula formation across related urogenital conditions is essential for accurate diagnosis and appropriate management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 2530
retrieved_docs.content: Plastic repair of urethrocele in female is a surgical intervention that is performed directly on the vagina, and its direct morphology is described as “انسدال,” indicating an obstructive or closure component. Posterior repair of vagina is another procedure that is specifically related to the vagina, emphasizing the organ’s central role in pelvic reconstructive surgery. Vesicovaginal fistula with involvement of the urinary continence mechanism following normal delivery is a pathological condition whose finding site is the vagina, highlighting that defects of the urinary continence system after childbirth are localized to this same anatomical region. Together, these concepts illustrate how both therapeutic procedures (plastic repair of urethrocele and posterior vaginal repair) and disease processes (vesicovaginal fistula affecting continence) are interconnected through their focus on the vagina, underscoring the importance of detailed anatomical and morphological understanding—such as recognizing obstruction (“انسدال”)—for effective patient assessment and management.
